ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general salmon food product class; no specific teriyaki salmon fillet class exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated teriyaki salmon code. A00GJ (cooked salmon) is the closest defensible base code. Teriyaki sauce facet is not separately enumerated.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for teriyaki salmon fillet identified in clinical terminology; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of teriyaki salmon fillet is approximately 150 g, representing one standard restaurant or home-cooked fillet portion.

nutrient_source.title: Teriyaki Salmon — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database, Food Item F03020033
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F03020033 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros and secondary nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are taken from this approved primary source. Saturated fat (3.86 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (6.33 g/100g), and polyunsaturated fat (5.14 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, cooked Atlantic salmon (NDB 15236), as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid breakdown for this entry. This adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index — Not Applicable: Negligible Carbohydrate Content. Reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Teriyaki salmon fillet contains only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100g, the majority of which derives from the teriyaki glaze. As a high-protein, high-fat fish dish with negligible net carbohydrate content, glycemic index is not applicable per Project Veritas rules for low-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) do not assign GI values to protein- and fat-dominant foods with negligible carbohydrate. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable. GL per serving is 0.0 to reflect negligible glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 359 mg/100g with very low potassium (18 mg/100g) and negligible dietary fibre (0.05 g/100g), providing no meaningful offset to sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium soy sauce in the teriyaki marinade, as the sodium content of 359 mg/100g is in the borderline-high range and the very low potassium (18 mg/100g, well below the 200 mg/100g offset threshold) provides no meaningful counterbalance; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or sweet potato is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (2.5 g/100g), low sugar (1.61 g/100g), and high protein (21.1 g/100g) strongly support glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Teriyaki salmon fillet is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions, as the low carbohydrate content (2.5 g/100g) and high protein (21.1 g/100g) minimise postprandial glucose excursion; however, excessive teriyaki sauce should be avoided as larger quantities increase sugar and sodium load, and the dish should be paired with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 PUFA profile from salmon partially offset by elevated sodium (359 mg/100g) from teriyaki soy sauce marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon is a rich source of cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), and the saturated fat content (~3.86 g/100g) is below the 5 g/100g threshold of concern; however, the sodium content of 359 mg/100g from the teriyaki soy sauce marinade is a meaningful cardiovascular risk factor, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sodium preparation and limit portion frequency.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-3 PUFA content (EPA and DHA) from salmon directly reduces joint inflammation; saturated fat well below 5 g/100g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Salmon is an excellent source of anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), making teriyaki salmon fillet beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; however, the soy sauce in the teriyaki marinade contributes sodium that can worsen RA-related tissue swelling, so reduced-sodium soy sauce is recommended, and charring during grilling should be avoided as advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) are pro-inflammatory.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teriyaki salmon fillet is a grilled salmon dish coated in a sweet-savoury teriyaki glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The salmon fillet is marinated and then grilled or pan-seared until the glaze caramelises, producing a lacquered, slightly sticky surface with a tender, flaky interior. The flavour profile is umami-rich with a mild sweetness from the mirin and sugar, balanced by the saltiness of soy sauce. Teriyaki is a Japanese cooking technique (照り焼き) in which foods are broiled or grilled with a glaze of soy sauce, mirin, and sugar; the technique is widely adopted in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, teriyaki salmon fillet is high in protein (21.1 g/100g) and total fat (16.92 g/100g), with a favourable fatty acid profile dominated by anti-inflammatory monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats including EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids. Carbohydrate content is low at 2.5 g/100g, making this dish suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is elevated at 359 mg/100g due to the soy sauce component of the teriyaki marinade, which is a clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is clinically relevant as a high-protein, omega-3-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and cardiovascular disease, subject to sodium management. Energy density is 246 kcal/100g.

Can I eat teriyaki salmon if I have high blood pressure?
What is teriyaki salmon made of?
How much protein in teriyaki salmon?
Is teriyaki salmon good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Input record is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F03020033, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed, provenance canonical). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this approved primary source. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked Atlantic salmon (NDB 15236) as HPB does not report these fields for this entry; this adaptation is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.86 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (6.33 g/100g), and polyunsaturated fat (5.14 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, cooked Atlantic salmon (NDB 15236), as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid breakdown for this entry. These values are approximate and may differ from the actual teriyaki-prepared product due to sauce contribution. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported by HPB Foodo for this specific teriyaki salmon fillet entry (F03020033), and no direct approved-source equivalent with complete micronutrient data for this preparation was identified in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or other approved sources as of 2026-04-17. 3. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100g by HPB; this is an unusually low value for a cooked salmon product (USDA reports approximately 63 mg/100g for cooked Atlantic salmon). This discrepancy is noted; the HPB value is preserved as the primary approved source, but the value should be verified in a future review cycle. 4. Potassium is reported as 18 mg/100g by HPB, which is atypically low for salmon (USDA reports approximately 490 mg/100g for cooked Atlantic salmon). This value is preserved from the approved HPB source but flagged for verification. 5. Glycemic index is not applicable: teriyaki salmon fillet contains only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100g; GI is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Project Veritas rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care, 2008) do not assign GI values to protein- and fat-dominant foods with negligible carbohydrate. 6. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as salmon and teriyaki sauce do not contain industrially produced trans fats; this is confirmed zero by formulation. 7. The dish is classified as Japanese cuisine based on the teriyaki cooking technique (照り焼き), which originated in Japan, though it is widely consumed in Singapore and internationally. Multilingual names are provided in Japanese and Chinese; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean-origin dish.

Diabetes Care 2008 reference as the approved GI authority confirming that GI is not assigned to negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_source organization is 'University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)'. It explains what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context (Japanese teriyaki technique), clinical relevance (diabetes, hypertension, CVD, RA), and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms are populated.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' is correctly used. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis is included from input.
